title: EXPOSÉ XVIII COHOMOLOGICAL STUDY OF LEFSCHETZ PENCILS

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XVIII COHOMOLOGICAL STUDY OF LEFSCHETZ PENCILS

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XVIII
COHOMOLOGICAL STUDY OF LEFSCHETZ PENCILSby N. Katz (*)
CONTENTS

1.The cohomology of a projective bundle.2
2.The cohomology of blown-up varieties.4
3.The blow-up associated with a pencil.13
4.The cohomology of the blow-up associated with a pencil.17
5.Cohomological study of the morphism \(p:\widetilde X\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\) associated with a pencil.24
5.1. The restriction homomorphism \(H^*(\widetilde X)\longrightarrow H^*(Y)\).24
5.2. The "vache Lefschetz theorem" (LV).27
5.3. The condition \(A\).33
5.8. The Griffiths homomorphism.47
5.9. An example where the hypothesis \(A\) is not satisfied.57
6.Applications of the local theory of vanishing cycles (Picard-Lefschetz).58
INTRODUCTION

In the present exposé we give the elementary cohomological structure of Lefschetz pencils. Sections 1–4 give the "well-known" facts on the cohomological structure of projective bundles and blow-ups. In 5 we make the study which appears in the title, assuming that \(X\) satisfies the "vache Lefschetz theorem" (5.2), and that the pencil \(D\) in question satisfies a certain condition \(A\) of local nature on \(D\) (5.3). The principal results are 5.6, 5.7 and 5.8.7, the last of which intervenes in an essential way in the proof of the theorem of GRIFFITHS in XX. In the last section, of a distinctly less elementary nature, we rely on the exposé SGA 1 XIII of Mme RAYNAUD on the "tame" fundamental group, and on the Picard-Lefschetz formula (XVI) to prove:

1. (1) The condition \(A\) is often satisfied (6.3, 6.4);
  2. (2) Except in the case \(\dim(X)\) odd and characteristic = 2, the monodromy group of a Lefschetz pencil acts irreducibly on the "vanishing cohomology", and transitively (modulo sign) on the set of
"local vanishing cycles" associated (via the Picard-Lefschetz formula) with the various "critical points" of the pencil (6.6 and 6.7).

pages.title: 1. The cohomology of a projective bundle

blocks.html: 1. The cohomology of a projective bundle

blocks.title: Theorem 1.1. (DELIGNE).
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.1. (DELIGNE).

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) be a proper morphism, \(\ell\) a prime number invertible on \(S\), \(u\in H^2(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\) (resp. \(u\in H^2(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(1))\)) a cohomology class on \(X\), and \(\mathcal F\) a \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf (resp. \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaf) on \(X\). We suppose that there exists an integer \(n\) such that for every integer \(j\) and every integer \(i>0\), the map

\[ \tag{1.1.1}R^{n-i}f_*(\mathcal F(j))\xrightarrow{\ \wedge u^i\ } R^{n+i}f_*(\mathcal F(j+i)), \]

is an isomorphism of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves (resp. of \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves) on \(S\). Then for every morphism \(g:S\longrightarrow T\), the Leray spectral sequence

\[ E_2^{p,q}=R^pg_*R^qf_*(\mathcal F)\Longrightarrow R^{p+q}(gf)_*(\mathcal F) \]

is degenerate.
The proof is found in [1, Prop. 2.4]. One will remark that, thanks to the base change theorem for a proper morphism (SGA 4 XII 5.2), the hypothesis can be verified fiber by fiber.

blocks.title: Theorem 1.2.
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.2. (Cf. SGA 5 VII 2.2.1).

Let \(\mathcal E\longrightarrow X\) be a vector bundle, locally free of rank \(1+r\). Let us denote by \(f:\mathbb P(\mathcal E)\longrightarrow X\) the projective bundle defined by \(\mathcal E\), and by \(u\in H^2(\mathbb P(\mathcal E),\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\) the first Chern class of the canonical invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P(\mathcal E)}(1)\). Then, for \(\ell\) a prime number invertible on \(X\), we have

a) the spectral sequence

\[ \tag{1.2.1}E_2^{p,q}=H^p(X,R^qf_*\mathbb Z_\ell)\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\mathbb P(\mathcal E),\mathbb Z_\ell) \]

degenerates.

b) the arrow

\[ \tag{1.2.2}\bigoplus_{0\leq j\leq r}H^{q-2j}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(-j)) \longrightarrow H^q(\mathbb P(\mathcal E),\mathbb Z_\ell) \]

defined by \(\bigoplus\tau_{q-2j}\longmapsto\sum f^*(\tau_{q-2j})\wedge u^j\) is an isomorphism.
Proof. The fibers of \(f\) being projective spaces of dimension \(r\), we know that

\[ \tag{1.2.3}R^if_*\mathbb Z_\ell\xleftarrow[\sim]{\ \wedge u^{i/2}\ } \begin{cases} \mathbb Z_\ell\left(-\dfrac i2\right)&\text{for \(i\) even, \(0\leq i\leq2r\),}\\ 0&\text{otherwise.} \end{cases} \]
Theorem 1.1 applies to the situation \((f,\ell,u,\mathbb Z_\ell,r)\), whence a). By 1.2.3 we have
\[ \tag{1.2.4}E_2^{a,b}= \begin{cases} H^a(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(-b/2))&\text{for \(b\) even, \(0\leq b\leq2r\),}\\ 0&\text{otherwise,} \end{cases} \]

hence the associated graded of \(H^q(\mathbb P(\mathcal E),\mathbb Z_\ell)\) for the filtration coming from the spectral sequence is \(\displaystyle\bigoplus_{0\leq j\leq r}H^{q-2j}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(-j))\). One sees at once that, equipping \(\displaystyle\bigoplus_{0\leq j\leq r}H^{q-2j}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(-j))\) with the filtration
\[ F^i=\bigoplus_{\substack{0\leq j\leq r\\q-2j\leq i}}H^{q-2j}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(-j)), \]
the arrow 1.2.2 is compatible with the filtrations, and that it induces the identity on the associated graded objects, whence b).

pages.title: 2. The cohomology of blown-up varieties (cf. SGA 5 VII 8)

blocks.html: 2. The cohomology of blown-up varieties (cf. SGA 5 VII 8)

2.1. Let \(X\) be a scheme smooth over a field \(k\), and \(\Delta\subset X\) a smooth scheme everywhere of codimension \(1+r\). We know (EGA IV 19.4.9) that there exists a smooth scheme \(\widetilde X\), equipped with a projective morphism
\[ \tag{2.1.1}f:\widetilde X\longrightarrow X \]
such that, setting
\[ \tag{2.1.2}\widetilde\Delta=f^{-1}(\Delta)=\Delta\mathop{\times}_X\widetilde X, \]
\(f\) induces an isomorphism
\[ \tag{2.1.3}f/\widetilde X-\widetilde\Delta:\widetilde X-\widetilde\Delta\xrightarrow{\sim}X-\Delta, \]
and that there exists a \(\Delta\)-isomorphism between \(\widetilde\Delta\) and \(\mathbb P(N_{\Delta/X})\), the projective bundle
of lines in the normal bundle \(N_{\Delta/X}\) of \(\Delta\) in \(X\). We thus have a commutative diagram:
\[ \tag{2.1.4}\xymatrix{ \widetilde\Delta\ar[rr]^{\sim}\ar[dr]_{f|\widetilde\Delta}&&\mathbb P(N_{\Delta/X})\ar[dl]^{\text{canonical proj.}}\\ &\Delta } \]

2.1.5. In the special case \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\Delta=\operatorname{Spec}(A/I)\) where \(I=(g_0,\ldots,g_r)\), \(\widetilde X\) is obtained by the Hopf procedure (cf. EGA IV 19.4.11): We define a morphism from \(X-\Delta\) to \(\mathbb P^r\) by
\[ x\in X-\Delta\longmapsto(g_0(x),\ldots,g_r(x))\in\mathbb P^r, \]

and we consider its graph \(\Gamma\) as embedded in \(X\times\mathbb P^r\). Then we define \(\widetilde X\) = the schematic closure of \(\Gamma\) in \(X\times\mathbb P^r\), and the morphism \(f:\widetilde X\longrightarrow X\) is obtained as the composite
\[ \widetilde X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\times\mathbb P^r\xrightarrow{\operatorname{pr}_1}X. \]

2.1.6. Analogously (cf. again EGA IV 19.4.11), if \(X\) is projective, and if \(\Delta\) is the intersection of \(X\) with \(1+r\) hypersurfaces, say with equations \(G_i=0\), \(i=0,\ldots,r\), we again consider the morphism
\[ X-\Delta\longrightarrow\mathbb P^r, \qquad x\in X-\Delta\longmapsto(G_0(x),\ldots,G_r(x)), \]
and we obtain \(\widetilde X\) as the closure of its graph in \(X\times\mathbb P^r\). In concrete
terms, in terms of the homogeneous coordinates \(\lambda_0,\ldots,\lambda_r\) in \(\mathbb P^r\), \(\widetilde X\) is obtained as the scheme of \(X\times\mathbb P^r\) defined by the equations
\[ \tag{2.1.6.1}\lambda_iG_j(x)-\lambda_jG_i(x)=0\qquad 0\leq i<j\leq r. \]
One sees from these equations that the point \((x,\lambda)\) lies in \(\widetilde\Delta\) if and only if \(x\in\Delta\); in other words, \(\mathbb P(N_{\Delta/X})\) is the trivial projective bundle
\[ \tag{2.1.6.2}\xymatrix{ \mathbb P(N_{\Delta/X})\ar[rr]^{\sim}\ar[dr]_{\text{canonical proj.}}&&\Delta\times\mathbb P^r\ar[dl]^{\operatorname{pr}_1}\\ &\Delta } \]
We have the following commutative diagram

\[ \tag{2.1.6.3}\xymatrix{ \Delta\times\mathbb P^r\ar[r]^{\sim}\ar[dr]_{\operatorname{pr}_1}&\widetilde\Delta\ar@{^{(}->}[r]\ar[d]^{f|\widetilde\Delta}&\widetilde X\ar@{^{(}->}[r]\ar[d]^f&X\times\mathbb P^r\ar[dl]^{\operatorname{pr}_1}\\ &\Delta\ar@{^{(}->}[r]&X, } \]
and the composite inclusion \(\Delta\times\mathbb P^r\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\times\mathbb P^r\) comes from \(\Delta\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\) by base change.

blocks.title: Theorem 2.2.
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.2.

Let \(X\) be smooth over a field \(k\), \(\Delta\) a smooth subscheme everywhere of codimension \(1+r\), and \(f:\widetilde X\longrightarrow X\) the blow-up of \(X\) at \(\Delta\). Then:

(2.2.1) The Leray spectral sequence

\[ E_2^{p,q}=H^p(X,R^qf_*\mathbb Z_\ell)\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell) \]

degenerates.

2.2.2. There exists an additive isomorphism

\[ H^q(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\xleftarrow{\sim} H^q(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^q(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)/\operatorname{Im}H^q(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell) \xleftarrow{\sim} \bigoplus_{j=1}^rH^{q-2j}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-j))\oplus H^q(X,\mathbb Z_\ell). \]
Proof. Let us denote by
\[ \tag{2.2.3}g:\widetilde\Delta\longrightarrow\Delta \]
the restriction of \(f\) to \(\widetilde\Delta\), and by
\[ \tag{2.2.4}\left\{\begin{aligned} j:&\ \Delta\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\\ i:&\ \widetilde\Delta\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\widetilde X \end{aligned}\right. \]
the inclusions, so as to obtain the commutative diagram
\[ \tag{2.2.5}\xymatrix{ \widetilde X&\widetilde\Delta\ar[l]_i\\ X\ar[u]^f&\Delta\ar[l]^j\ar[u]_g } \]
The latter induces a homomorphism of the Leray spectral sequences for the morphisms \(f\) and \(g\), and the sheaf \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) on \(\widetilde X\) and \(\widetilde\Delta\),
\[ \tag{2.2.6}E_r^{p,q}(f)\xrightarrow{(i^*,j^*)}E_r^{p,q}(g). \]
On the other hand, \(f\) inducing an isomorphism of \(\widetilde X-\widetilde\Delta\) with \(X-\Delta\), we have, by the base change theorem for a proper morphism,
\[ \tag{2.2.7}R^qf_*\mathbb Z_\ell\simeq \begin{cases} \mathbb Z_\ell&\text{if }q=0,\\ j_*R^qg_*\mathbb Z_\ell&\text{if }q>0, \end{cases} \]
whence
\[ \tag{2.2.8}E_2^{p,q}(f)\xrightarrow{(i^*,j^*)}E_2^{p,q}(g)\qquad\text{for }q\ne0. \]
We shall prove by induction on \(r\) that
\[ \tag{2.2.9}E_r^{p,q}(f)\xrightarrow{(i^*,j^*)}E_r^{p,q}(g)qquad\text{for }q\ne0, r\geq2 \]
and
\[ \tag{2.2.10}d_r^{p,q}(f)=0\qquad\text{for }r\geq2. \]
Let us admit for the moment the formula
\[ \tag{2.2.11}d_r^{p,r-1}(f)=0\qquad\text{for }r\geq2,\ \text{all }p. \]
Let us begin with \(r=2\). We have a commutative diagram

For \(q\leq0\), we have \(E_2^{p+2,q-1}(f)=0\), hence \(d_2^{p,q}(f)=0\). For \(q=1\), we have \(d_2^{p,1}(f)=0\) by (2.2.11), and for \(q>1\), the horizontal arrows in (2.2.12) are isomorphisms, and we know that \(d_2^{p,q}(g)=0\) by 1.2 and 2.1.4. This gives (2.2.10) for \(r=2\), and (2.2.9) for \(r=2\) is none other than (2.2.8).

Suppose (2.2.9) and (2.2.10) known for a value of \(r\geq2\). The hypothesis (2.2.10) implies \(E_r^{p,q}(f)\simeq E_{r+1}^{p,q}(f)\), and by 1.2 we have \(E_r^{p,q}(g)\simeq E_{r+1}^{p,q}(g)\), so that we have the commutative diagram
which gives (2.2.9) for (r+1), taking into account (2.2.9) for \(r\). To deduce (2.2.10) for (r+1), we look at the commutative diagram

For \(q\leq r-1\), we have \(E_{r+1}^{p+r+1,q-r}(f)=0\), hence \(d_r^{p,q}(f)=0\). For \(q=r\), we have (2.2.11), and for \(q>r\) the horizontal arrows in (2.2.14) are isomorphisms, and \(d_{r+1}^{p,q}(g)=0\) by 1.2, whence the desired conclusion.
It remains to verify (2.2.11), which means that the composite "edge homomorphism"
\[ \tag{2.2.15}E_2^{p,0}(f)\longrightarrow E_3^{p,0}(f)\longrightarrow E_4^{p,0}(f)\longrightarrow\cdots E_\infty^{p,0}(f) \]
is an isomorphism, i.e. that
\[ \tag{2.2.16}f^*:H^p(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\longrightarrow H^p(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell) \]
is injective. Now, there exists a left inverse, namely the Gysin homomorphism (SGA 5 IV)
\[ \tag{2.2.17}f_*:H^p(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\longrightarrow H^p(X,\mathbb Z_\ell), \]
which indeed satisfies,
\[ \tag{2.2.18}f_*f^*=\operatorname{id}. \]
as follows from the projection formula, and from the fact that \(f_*(1)=1\), \(f\) being birational hence of degree one.
This proves (2.2.1). As for (2.2.2), let us remark that the diagram (2.2.5) gives, on passing to cohomology, a commutative diagram

\[ \tag{2.2.19}\xymatrix@C=5em{ H^p(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[r]^{i^*}&H^p(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)\\ H^p(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[u]^{f^*}\ar[r]_{j^*}&H^p(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[u]_{g^*}}, \]
whence a morphism
\[ \tag{2.2.20} \frac{H^p(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)}{f^*H^p(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)} \xrightarrow{(i^*,j^*)} \frac{H^p(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)}{g^*H^p(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)}. \]

Let us denote by \(F^i\) the filtrations of \(H^i(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\) and of \(H^i(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)\) coming from the Leray spectral sequences, so that (2.2.20) can be rewritten

\[ \tag{2.2.21} \frac{H^p(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)}{F^pH^p(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)} \xrightarrow{(i^*,j^*)} \frac{H^p(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)}{H^pF^p(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)}. \]
Now, by (2.2.9), the associated graded of the arrow (2.2.21) is an iso- morphism, hence (2.2.20) is an isomorphism. On the other hand, (2.2.18) gives a direct sum decomposition
\[ \tag{2.2.22} H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell) \xleftarrow[,f_*\oplus(1-f^*f_*),]{,f^*+\mathrm{inclusion},} H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus\operatorname{Ker}f_*, \]
and (1.2.2) gives us a direct sum decomposition
\[ \tag{2.2.23} \frac{H^*(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)}{g^*H^*(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)} \xleftarrow{\sim} \bigoplus_{1\leq j\leq r}H^{*-2j}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-j)); \]
putting together the isomorphism (2.2.20) and the decompositions (2.2.22) and (2.2.23), we find (2.2.2), qed.

Remark 2.2.24. Let us sketch a second proof of (2.2.2), distinctly simpler, valid in the case where \(X\) is proper over \(k\). More generally, one finds (without hypothesis on \(X\)) the corresponding result (2.2.26) in cohomology with proper support. We have the exact sequences of "cohomology with proper supports", denoted \(H_c^*\), for the pairs \((\widetilde X,\widetilde\Delta)\) and \((X,\Delta)\) which form a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=2.6em{ (2.2.25)\ar[r]&H_c^i(\widetilde X-\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[r]&H_c^i(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[r]&H_c^i(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[r]&{H_c^{i+1}(\widetilde X-\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)}\\ (2.2.26)\ar[r]&H_c^i(X-\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[u]^{\sim}\ar[r]&H_c^i(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[u]^{f^*}\ar[r]&H_c^i(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[u]^{g^*}\ar[r]&{H_c^{i+1}(X-\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)}\ar[u]^{\sim}}. \]
Now, the quotient of the acyclic complex (2.2.25) by the acyclic subcomplex (2.2.26) gives us the acyclic complex

\[ \tag{2.2.27}\longrightarrow0\longrightarrow \frac{H_c^i(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)}{f^*H_c^i(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)} \longrightarrow \frac{H_c^i(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)}{g^*H_c^i(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)} \longrightarrow0\longrightarrow \]
i.e., we find, without considering spectral sequences, that (2.2.20) is an isomorphism. From then on, the arguments just given apply to yield (2.2.2).

blocks.title: Corollary 2.3.
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3. Let us set
\[ b_q(X)=\dim_{\mathbb Q_\ell}H^q(X,\mathbb Q_\ell),\qquad \chi(X)=\sum_q(-1)^qb_q(X). \]

We have

\[ \tag{2.3.1}b_q(\widetilde X)=b_q(X)+\sum_{j=1}^r b_{q-2j}(\Delta), \] \[ \tag{2.3.2}\chi(\widetilde X)=\chi(X)+r\,\chi(\Delta). \]

pages.title: 3. The blow-up associated with a pencil

blocks.html: 3. The blow-up associated with a pencil

3.1. Let \(X\) be projective, smooth and irreducible over an alge- braically closed field \(k\), of dimension \(n\), and \(D=(H_\tau)_{\tau\in\mathbb P^1}\) a pencil of hyper- surfaces whose axis cuts \(X\) transversally, in \(\Delta\). Let us then consider the blow-up of \(X\) at \(\Delta\), which we shall describe explicitly.

In terms of equations, denoting by \((\lambda,\mu)\) homogeneous coordinates of \(\mathbb P^1\), and by \(F\) and \(G\) two linear forms such that \(F=G=0\) de- fines the axis of the pencil, the pencil is written
\[ \tag{3.1.1}\lambda F-\mu G. \]
The subscheme \(\Delta\) is by definition the intersection of \(X\) with the linear subvariety \(G=F=0\). By 2.1.6,
\[ \tag{3.1.2}\widetilde X=\{(x,(\lambda,\mu))\in X\times\mathbb P^1\mid \lambda F-\mu G=0\}\ ; \]
it is the schematic closure, in \(X\times\mathbb P^1\), of the graph of the map
\[ \tag{3.1.3}\begin{aligned} X-\Delta&\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\\ x&\longmapsto(G(x),F(x)). \end{aligned} \]

The great virtue of \(\widetilde X\) is that it is a projective variety (being embedded in \(X\times\mathbb P^1\)) and smooth (since \(\Delta\) is) over \(k\), which admits a morphism \(\rho\) to \(\mathbb P^1\), namely
\[ \tag{3.1.4}\rho:\widetilde X\longrightarrow X\times\mathbb P^1\xrightarrow{\operatorname{pr}_2}\mathbb P^1, \]
whose fibers are the hyperplane sections of \(X\) by the members of our pencil
\[ \rho^{-1}(t)=X_t\overset{\mathrm{déf}}{=}X.H_t. \]
3.2. The degree of the dual variety: topological method.
Suppose in addition that the pencil of 3.1 is such that
(3.2.1) \(X_t\) is smooth for every \(t\) contained in a non-empty open set of \(\mathbb P^1\),

(3.2.2) If \(X_t\) is singular, it has only non-degenerate quadratic
singularities (XVII 1.1);
these conditions are satisfied in particular for a Lefschetz pencil (XVII 2.21) if \(\operatorname{car}k\ne2\) or if \(X\) is of even dimension.

Let \(\eta\) be the generic point of \(\mathbb P^1\), \(\bar\eta\) a geometric point above it, \(n\) the dimension of \(X\), and \(\chi\) the Euler-Poincaré characteristic with values in the sheaf \(\mathbb Q_\ell\). Then the Euler-Poincaré formula XVI 2.1 applied to the morphism \(S\) gives us
\[ \tag{3.2.3}\chi(\widetilde X)=\chi(\mathbb P^1)\times\chi(X_{\bar\eta})+(-1)^n \sum_{\substack{\text{sing. pts}\\\text{of the fibers}}}1, \]
a formula which, taking into account (2.2.2), can be rewritten
\[ \tag{3.2.4}(-1)^n[\text{total number of singular points}] =\chi(X)+\chi(\Delta)-2\chi(X_{\bar\eta}). \]

One will remark that the quantity \(\chi(X)+\chi(\Delta)-2\chi(X_{\bar\eta})\) is independent of the particular choice of the pencil, and depends only on the embedding of \(X\) in a \(\mathbb P^r\), and even only on the Chern class of \(\mathcal O_X(1)\), cf. [XVII, 5.7.5 and 5.7.6].

3.2.5. Let us make this explicit in a concrete case. Let us take \(X=\mathbb P^2\), \(\{H_t\}\) a pencil of curves of degree \(d\) in \(\mathbb P^2\) which satisfies (3.1), (3.2.1) and (3.2.2). We have
\[ \tag{3.2.6}\begin{aligned} \chi(X)&=3 &&\text{(by virtue of 1.2)}\\ \chi(X_{\bar\eta})&=-d(d-3) &&\text{(cf. SGA 5 VII 7.3)}\\ \chi(\Delta)&=d^2, \end{aligned} \]
whence
\[ \tag{3.2.7}\text{the total number of singular points is }3(d-1)^2. \]
Let us take the particular case \(d=3\), and consider the family of elliptic curves (in characteristic \(\ne3\))
The four singular fibers lie above \(\mu=\) a cube root of unity, and \(\mu=\infty\), and each singular fiber is the union of three lines like this
\[ \tag{3.2.9}\begin{array}{c} \diagup\!\!\!\!\diagdown\\[-0.8em] \rule{5em}{0.4pt} \end{array}, \]
and has exactly three singular points, all quadratic non-degenerate. That makes twelve singular points in all, and verifies (3.2.7) in this example.

blocks.title: Proposition 3.2.10.
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.2.10. Let \(X\hookrightarrow\mathbb P^r\) be a Lefschetz embedding (XVII 2.3), and suppose that \(n=\dim X\) is even, or that \(\operatorname{car}k\ne2\). Then every Lefschetz pencil, considered as a line in \(\check{\mathbb P}^r\), cuts \(\check X\) in
\[ \tag{3.2.11}(-1)^n[\chi(X)+\chi(\Delta)-2\chi(X_{\bar\eta})] \]

points, and cuts it transversally (EGA IV 17.13.7).

Proof. By hypothesis, every singularity of a fiber of the Lefschetz pencil is quadratic non-degenerate, so that (3.2.4) applies to give that the number of singular fibers (each containing exactly one singular point) in the pencil \(D\) is the number (3.2.11).

Now, the fiber \(X_\tau\) is singular if and only if \(\tau\in\check X\cap D\). To see the transversality of the intersection, one remarks that the number (3.2.11) is independent of the particular choice of the Lefschetz pencil, and that, the generic pencil being a Lefschetz pencil, this number (3.2.11) is the degree of \(\check X\) (i.e. its degree as a hypersurface in \(\check{\mathbb P}^r\), which is (by convention) 0 if \(\dim\check X\le r-2\)). Now, if a line \(D\) cuts a hyper- surface of degree \(d>0\) in \(d\) distinct points \(x_i\) (\(1\le i\le d\)), the intersection is necessarily transversal. Indeed, the intersection multiplicity \(\mu_i=\operatorname{rg}(D\cap\check X|x_i)\) of \(D\) and \(\check X\) at an \(x_i\) is \(\ge1\), so as \(\sum_1^d\mu_i=d\), we have \(\mu_i=1\), for every \(i\), i.e. \(D\cap\check X\) is smooth, which is equivalent to transversality, as one sees at once.

Remark 3.2.12. For a Lefschetz pencil \(D\) which is already assumed to cut \(\check X\) transversally (a condition verified in any case for \(D\) general enough), the result 3.2.10 can also be considered as the conjunction of XVII 5.7.2 and of the fact that \(\deg\varphi=1\), contained in XVII 3.5. Of course, the fact that every Lefschetz pencil \(D\) cuts \(\check X\) transversally, established here via the Euler- Poincaré formula XVI 2.1, can also be verified directly in an elementary way, using the fact that the axis of \(D\) cuts \(X\) transversally.

pages.title: 4. The cohomology of the blow-up associated with a pencil

blocks.html: 4. The cohomology of the blow-up associated with a pencil
4.1. We return to the situation 3.1, summarized by the commu- tative diagram (cf. 2.1.6.3):

\[ \tag{4.1.1}\xymatrix@C=3.2em@R=3em{ X\times\mathbb P^1&\widetilde X\ar[l]_{f\times\rho}&\widetilde\Delta\ar[l]_i\ar[r]^{(g,\rho_i)}&\Delta\times\mathbb P^1\\ \mathbb P^1&\widetilde X\ar[l]_\rho\ar[u]^{f\times\rho}\ar[d]^f&&\widetilde\Delta\ar[u]_{(g,\rho_i)}\ar[d]^g\\ &&X&\Delta\ar[l]^j } \]
Let us begin by making (2.2.2) precise in this case.

blocks.title: Proposition 4.2.
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2. Under the hypotheses of 3.1:

(4.2.1) The homomorphisms of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules

\[ H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell) \xleftarrow[,f^*+\mathrm{ind},]{\sim,f_*\oplus(1-f^*f_*)} H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus\operatorname{Ker}\bigl(f_*:H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\to H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\bigr) \]

are isomorphisms, inverse to each other.

(4.2.2) The homomorphisms of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules

\[ H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell) \xrightarrow{\ f_*\oplus(g_*i^*)\ } H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)) \xrightarrow{\ f^*+i_*g^*\ }H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell) \]

are isomorphisms, inverse to each other.

(4.2.3) The algebra structure on \(H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\) deduced by "transport of structure" via (4.2.2) from the cup-product in \(H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\), is expressed, in terms of the elements

\[a,b\in H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell),\quad x,y\in H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)),\]

by the formulas:

\[\tag{4.2.3.1}(0\oplus x)\wedge(0\oplus y)=-j_*(xy)\oplus2xy\,c_1(\mathcal O_\Delta(1)),\] \[\tag{4.2.3.2}(a\oplus0)\wedge(b\oplus0)=ab\oplus0,\] \[\tag{4.2.3.3}(a\oplus0)\wedge(0\oplus y)=0\oplus j^*(a)y,\] \[\tag{4.2.3.4}(0\oplus x)\wedge(b\oplus0)=0\oplus xj^*(b).\]

In particular, for

\[a\oplus x\in H^i(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{i-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)),\] \[b\oplus y\in H^{2n-i}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{2n-2-i}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)),\]

we have

\[\tag{4.2.3.5}(a\oplus x)\wedge(b\oplus y)=ab-j_*(xy).\]

In other words, the total intersection matrix of \(H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\) is written:

\[ \left(\begin{array}{c|c} \begin{array}{c}\text{the total matrix}\\\text{of the intersections on }X\end{array}&0\\ \hline 0&-\begin{array}{c}\text{the total matrix}\\\text{of the intersections}\\\text{on }\Delta\end{array} \end{array}\right). \]
Proof. We have already proved (4.2.1); (cf. 2.2.22). For (4.2.2), let us first remark that, \(g:\widetilde\Delta\longrightarrow\Delta\) being a projective bundle of rank one, the sequence
\[\tag{4.2.4}0\longrightarrow H^*(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)\xrightarrow{g^*} H^*(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)\xrightarrow{g_*} H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\longrightarrow0\]
is exact (cf. (1.2.2)). Taking into account (2.2.26), we obtain the commutative diagram of exact sequences

\[ \tag{4.2.5}\xymatrix@C=2.2em@R=3em{ 0\ar[r]&H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[r]^{f^*}\ar[d]^{j^*}&H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[r]^{g_*i^*}\ar[d]^{i^*}&H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&0\\ 0\ar[r]&H^*(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[r]^{g^*}&H^*(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[r]^{g_*}&H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\ar[r]&0.} \]
Taking into account the splitting (4.2.1), this gives an isomorphism
\[\tag{4.2.6}f_*\oplus g_*i^*:H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\xrightarrow{\sim} H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)).\]
The transpose of (4.2.5) gives a commutative diagram of exact sequences (after the shift, \(\widetilde{\longrightarrow}\,2n-*\))

\[ \tag{4.2.7}\xymatrix@C=2em@R=3em{ 0&H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[l]&H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\ar[l]_{f_*}&H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\ar[l]_{i_*g^*}&0\ar[l]\\ 0&H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-2))\ar[l]\ar[u]^{j_*}&H^{*-2}(\widetilde\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\ar[l]_{g_*}\ar[u]^{i_*}&H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\ar[l]_{g^*}\ar@{=}[u]&0\ar[l].} \]
and we obtain an isomorphism
\[\tag{4.2.8}H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)) \xrightarrow{f^*+i_*g^*}H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell).\]
It remains to prove that the composite

\[\tag{4.2.9}H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)) \xrightarrow{f^*+i_*g^*}H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell) \xrightarrow{f_*\oplus(g_*i^*)}H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\]
is the identity. Now, we have
\[f_*f^*=\operatorname{id}\text{ on }H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell),\quad\text{by (2.2.18)},\] \[g_*i^*f^*=0\quad\text{by (4.2.5)},\qquad f_*i_*g^*=0\quad\text{by (4.2.7)},\]
so that it suffices to prove
\[\tag{4.2.10}g_*i^*i_*g^*(x)=x\quad\text{for }x\in H^*(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)).\]
To verify (4.2.10), let us first remark that, \(i:\widetilde\Delta\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\widetilde X\) being the inclusion of a smooth subvariety of codimension one, we have [SGA 5 VII]
\[\tag{4.2.11}i^*i_*(x)=c_1(N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X})x\] \[\tag{4.2.12}i_*(x)i_*(y)=i_*\bigl(xy\,c_1(N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X})\bigr).\]
Let us write \(\xi_X=\mathcal O_X(1)\), \(\xi_\Delta=\mathcal O_\Delta(1)\), \(\xi_{\mathbb P^1}=\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\), etc.
By virtue of EGA II 8.1.7, we have, if \(\widetilde{\mathcal J}\) is the Ideal \(\widetilde\Delta\) in \(\widetilde X\)

\[\tag{4.2.13}\check N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X}\simeq \widetilde{\mathcal J}/\widetilde{\mathcal J}^{2}\simeq \widetilde{\mathcal J}\otimes_{\mathcal O_{\widetilde X}}\mathcal O_{\widetilde\Delta} \simeq\xi_X(1)\otimes_{\mathcal O_{\widetilde X}}\mathcal O_{\widetilde\Delta} =\mathcal O_{\widetilde\Delta}(1),\]

where \(\widetilde\Delta\) is interpreted as \(\mathbb P(\mathcal J/\mathcal J^2)\), \(\mathcal J\) being the Ideal of \(\Delta\) in \(X\). Now we have a canonical isomorphism (\(\Delta\) being represented as the intersection of \(X\) with two given hyperplanes in \(\mathbb P^r\)),
\[\mathcal J/\mathcal J^2\simeq\mathcal O_\Delta^2(-1),\]
which gives rise to the isomorphism
\[\widetilde\Delta=\mathbb P(\mathcal J/\mathcal J^2)\simeq \mathbb P(\mathcal O_\Delta^2(-1))\xrightarrow{\alpha}\mathbb P(\mathcal O_\Delta^2) \simeq\Delta\times\mathbb P^1\]
where \(\alpha\) is the canonical isomorphism EGA II 4.1.4. By loc. cit. this isomorphism transforms

\[\mathcal O_{\widetilde\Delta}(1)=\mathcal O_{\mathbb P(\mathcal J/\mathcal J^2)}(1) =\mathcal O_{\mathbb P(\mathcal O_\Delta^2(-1))}(1)\quad\text{into}\quad \mathcal O_{\mathbb P(\mathcal O_\Delta^2)}(1)\otimes_{\mathcal O_\Delta^2} \mathcal O_\Delta(-1)=\xi_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\otimes_k\mathcal O_\Delta(-1),\]
so that (4.2.13) is written
\[\tag{4.2.14}\check N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X}\simeq (\rho_i)^*(\xi_{\mathbb P^1}(1))\otimes g^*(\mathcal O_\Delta(-1)),\]
whence, taking the \(c_1\) of both sides, the

blocks.title: Lemma 4.3.
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.3. We have
\[c_1(N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X}) =g^*(\xi_\Delta)-(\rho_i)^*(\xi_{\mathbb P^1}).\]
Let us resume the proof of (4.2.10). By (4.2.11) and (4.3), it suffices to prove
\[g_*\left(g^*(x)\left[(\rho_i)^*\bigl(c_1(\xi_{\mathbb P^1})\bigr) -g^*\bigl(c_1(\xi_\Delta)\bigr)\right]\right)=x.\]
Now, by (4.2.4), \(g_*g^*=0\), and, taking into account that
\[\widetilde\Delta\xrightarrow{\ g\times\rho_i\ }\Delta\times\mathbb P^1,\]
we have
\[\tag{4.3.1}g_*\left(g^*(x)(\rho_i)^*\bigl(c_1(\xi_{\mathbb P^1})\bigr)\right)=x\] \[\tag{4.3.2}g_*\left(g^*(x)c_1(N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X})\right)=-x.\]

Let us now calculate the algebra structure on \(H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\), deduced from the cup-product by transport of structure via (4.2.2). Let \(a,b\) be elements of \(H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\), \(x,y\) elements of \(H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\). Let us begin with (4.2.3.1).

\[(0\oplus x)\wedge(0\oplus y)=?\oplus ?',\] \[\begin{aligned} ?&=f_*\bigl((i_*g^*(x))(i_*g^*(y))\bigr)\\ &=f_*\bigl(i_*\bigl(g^*(xy)c_1(N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X})\bigr)\bigr) &&\text{by (4.2.12)}\\ &=j_*g_*\bigl(g^*(xy)c_1(N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X})\bigr)\\ &=j_*(-xy)&&\text{by (4.3.2)}. \end{aligned}\]

\[\begin{aligned} ?'&=-g_*i^*\bigl(i_*g^*(x)\wedge i_*g^*(y)\bigr)\\ &=-g_*i^*\bigl(i_*\bigl(g^*(xy)c_1(N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X})\bigr)\bigr) &&\text{by (4.3.2)}\\ &=-g_*\bigl(g^*(xy)(c_1(N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X}))^2\bigr) &&\text{by (4.3.2)}. \end{aligned}\]
now,

\[\begin{aligned} (c_1(N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X}))^2 &=\bigl(g^*c_1(\mathcal O_\Delta(1))-(\rho_i)^*(c_1(\xi_{\mathbb P^1}))\bigr)^2 &&\text{by (4.3)}\\ &=g^*((c_1(\xi_\Delta))^2)-2g^*(c_1(\xi_\Delta))(\rho_i)^*(c_1(\xi_{\mathbb P^1})) \end{aligned}\] \[\left(+\ (\rho_i)^*((c_1(\xi_{\mathbb P^1}))^2)=0\right),\] \[?'=-g_*\bigl(g^*(xy)g^*(c_1(\xi_\Delta)^2)\bigr) +g_*\bigl(2g^*(xy)g^*(c_1(\xi_\Delta))(\rho_i)^*(c_1(\xi_{\mathbb P^1}))\bigr)\] \[?=0+2xy\,c_1(\xi_\Delta)\qquad\text{by (4.2.4) and (4.3.1)},\]
which establishes (4.2.3.1). As for (4.2.3.2): \((a\oplus0)\wedge(b\oplus0)=ab\oplus0\), this expresses that \(f^*\) is an algebra homomorphism.
Let us prove (4.2.3.3). Now, the projection formula implies \(\operatorname{Ker}f_*\cap\operatorname{image}f^*\subset\operatorname{Ker}f_*\), so that
\[(a\oplus0)\wedge(0\oplus y)=0\oplus ?,\]
where

\[\begin{aligned} &=-g_*\bigl(i^*f^*(a)\,g^*(y)\,c_1(N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X})\bigr) &&\text{by (4.3.2)}\\ &=-g_*\bigl(g^*(j^*(a))\,g^*(y)\,c_1(N_{\widetilde\Delta/\widetilde X})\bigr)\\ &=j^*(a).y \end{aligned}\]
which establishes (4.2.3.3). One remarks finally that (4.2.3.4) is deduced from (4.2.3.3) by the anticommutativity of the cup-product.

pages.title: 5. Cohomological study of the morphism \(\rho:\widetilde X\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\) associated with a pencil

blocks.html: 5. Cohomological study of the morphism \(\rho:\widetilde X\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\) associated with a pencil
5.1. The restriction homomorphism \(H^*(\widetilde X)\longrightarrow H^*(Y)\).
Let us denote by \(Y\) one of the smooth fibers of \(\rho:\widetilde X\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\), say \(Y=\rho^{-1}(t_0)=X.H_{t_0}\). We have the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=2.5em@R=3em{ \mathbb P^1&\widetilde X\ar[l]_\rho\ar[d]^f&\widetilde\Delta\ar[l]_i\ar[r]^-\sim\ar[d]^g&\Delta\times\mathbb P^1\\ {\operatorname{Sp}(k)=\{t_0\}}\ar[u]&X&\Delta\ar@{_{(}->}[l]^j\ar[ur]_{\ell=\mathrm{id}\times\{t_0\}}\ar[dl]^h&\\ &Y\ar[ul]\ar@/_1.2pc/[uu]^(.3){k}\ar[u]_m&& } \qquad \ell=\operatorname{id}\times[t_0], \]
where \(f:\widetilde X\to X\), \(j:\Delta\to X\), and \(m:Y\to X\).

blocks.title: Proposition 5.1.1.
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1.1. Under the hypotheses of (3.1), \(Y\) denoting a smooth fiber:

(5.1.2) The restriction homomorphism

\[k^*:H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\longrightarrow H^*(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell)\]

is expressed \(via (4.2.2)\) by

\[H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)) \xrightarrow{\ m^*+h^*\ }H^*(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell).\]

(5.1.3) The Gysin arrow

\[k_*:H^{*-2}(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\longrightarrow H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\]

is expressed by

\[H^{*-2}(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\xrightarrow{\ m_*\oplus(-h^*)\ } H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)).\]
Proof. The smooth subschemes \(\widetilde\Delta\) and \(Y\) of \(\widetilde X\) cut each other transversally in \(\Delta\); in other words, the square of smooth schemes
\[ \tag{5.1.4}\xymatrix{ \Delta\ar[r]^\ell\ar[d]_h&\widetilde\Delta\ar[d]^i\\ Y\ar[r]_k&\widetilde X } \]
is cartesian, which implies the formula [SGA 5, IV ]
The composite
\[H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)) \xrightarrow{\ f^*+i_*g^*\ }H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell) \xrightarrow{\ k^*\ }H^*(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell)\]
is the sum of \(k^*f^*=m^*\) and of \(k^*i_*g^*=h_*\ell^*g^*\) by (5.1.5), and \(g.\ell=\operatorname{id}_\Delta\), which gives (5.1.2).
The composite
\[H^{*-2}(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\xrightarrow{\ k_*\ } H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\xrightarrow{\ f_*\oplus(g_*i^*)\ } H^*(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\oplus H^{*-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\]
is the sum of \(f_*k_*=m_*\) and of \(-g_*i^*k_*=-g_*\ell_*h^*=-h^*\), which gives (5.1.3).

blocks.title: Corollary 5.1.6.
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.1.6. Under the hypotheses of 5.1.1, for \(i\le n-1\), the images of
\[m^*:H^i(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\longrightarrow H^i(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell)\]

and of

\[k^*:H^i(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\longrightarrow H^i(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell)\]

are equal.
Proof. By the "weak Lefschetz theorem" on hyperplane sections (SGA 5 VII 7.1), for \(i\le n-1\) the arrow
\[\tag{5.1.7}H^{i-2}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\xrightarrow{\ j^*\ } H^{i-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\]
is an isomorphism, so that for \(i\le n-1\), the image of \(k^*\) is equal, thanks to (5.1.2), to the sum of that of \(m^*\) and of that of the composite
\[H^{i-2}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\xrightarrow{\ j^*\ } H^{i-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Z_\ell(-1))\xrightarrow{\ h^*\ }H^i(Y,\mathbb Z_\ell).\]
Now, by the projection formula
\[h_*j^*(x)=h_*\bigl(h^*(m^*(x))\bigr)=m^*(x)h_*(1)=\xi_Y\,m^*(x) =m^*(x\,\xi_X)\quad\text{since }m^*(\xi_X)=\xi_Y,\]
whence the inclusion \(\operatorname{Im}k^i\subset\operatorname{Im}m^i\), qed.
5.2. The "vache Lefschetz theorem" (LV)
5.2.1. Let \(S\) be projective of dimension \(n\), over the field \(k\) (alg. closed), equipped with a projective embedding \(S\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P\). Let us denote by
\[u\in H^2(S,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\]

the "cohomology class of a hyperplane section" (so that \(u\) is obtained as the inverse image under the embedding \(S\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P\) of the first Chern class of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(1)\), a class which lies in \(H^2(\mathbb P,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\)), and let us denote by
\[L_S:H^i(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\longrightarrow H^{i+2}(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j+1))\]
the operation "multiplication by \(u\)".
5.2.2. We say that \(S\) (equipped with \(u\)) satisfies (LV), (the "vache Lefschetz theorem") if for every (or one) integer \(j\) and every integer \(i>0\), the maps
\[L_S^i:H^{n-i}(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\longrightarrow H^{n+i}(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(i+j))\]
are isomorphisms.

5.2.2.1. It is conjectured that this condition is satisfied if \(S\) is smooth. It is true if \(k\) is of characteristic zero [6], from which one concludes, thanks to the "specialization theorem", SGA 4 XVI 2.2, that the same holds if \(S\), with its projective embedding, lifts to characteristic zero. Thus one finds that it is verified for \(S\) a smooth complete intersection. We also know that it is true for \(S\) smooth of dimension \(\leq2\) [2], and for \(S\) an abelian variety (either "directly" (LIEBERMAN [4] and [2]), or by the theorem of MUMFORD [5] which says that a polarized abelian variety lifts, with its polarization, to characteristic zero).
Let us recall that for a projective variety \(S\), one defines for \(i\leq n\) the "primitive part" of the cohomology:
\[ \tag{5.2.2.2}\operatorname{Prim}^i(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))= \operatorname{Ker}\bigl(L^{n-i+1}:H^i(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\longrightarrow H^{2n-i+2}(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j+n-i+1))\bigr). \]
If \(S\) satisfies (LV), we have for \(i\leq n\) the decomposition:
\[ \tag{5.2.2.3}H^i(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\xleftarrow[\sim]{\,1+L\,} \operatorname{Prim}^i(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\oplus H^{i-2}(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j-1)). \]

blocks.title: Proposition 5.2.3.
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.3. Let \(X\) be projective and smooth, satisfying (LV). Let us denote by \(\tau:Y\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\) the inclusion of a smooth hyperplane section. Then
\[ \tag{5.2.3.1}\operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))= \operatorname{Ker}\bigl(\tau^*:H^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\longrightarrow H^n(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\bigr). \]
Proof. We have, thanks to the projection formula for \(m:Y\longrightarrow X\), the commutative diagram
\[ \tag{5.2.3.2}\xymatrix@C=5em@R=4em{ H^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[r]^L\ar[dr]_{\tau^*}& H^{n+2}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j+1))\ar[dl]^{\tau_*}\\ &H^n(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)).} \]
By duality and weak Lefschetz,
\[ \tau_*:H^n(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\longrightarrow H^{n+2}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j+1)) \]
is the transpose of an isomorphism, hence an isomorphism, whence the conclusion.

blocks.title: Lemma 5.2.4.
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.4. Let \(X\) be projective and smooth, and \(\tau:Y\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\) the inclusion of a smooth hyperplane section. Then \(X\) satisfies (LV) if and only if the two following conditions are satisfied.

5.2.4.1. \(Y\) satisfies (LV).

5.2.4.2. The restriction of the cup-product \(H^{n-1}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) to the subspace \(\operatorname{Im}H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) is a non-degenerate form.
Proof. We have
\[ \tag{5.2.4.3}\left\{\begin{aligned} L_X&=\tau_*\tau^*,\\ L_Y&=\tau^*\tau_*\qquad\text{(by 4.2.11)}, \end{aligned}\right. \]
so that
In the commutative diagram

\[ \tag{5.2.4.5}\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3.5em{ H^{n-i-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[r]^{L_X^{i+1}}\ar[d]_{\tau^*}& H^{n+i+1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j+i+1))\\ H^{n-1-i}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[r]^{L_Y^i}& H^{n-1+i}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j+i))\ar[u]_{\tau_*}} \]
we know that \(\tau^*\) is an isomorphism for \(i\geq1\) by "weak Lefschetz", and that \(\tau_*\) is by duality. Hence \(X\) satisfies (LV) if and only if \(Y\) satisfies (LV), and the arrow
\[ \tag{5.2.4.6}L_X:H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\longrightarrow H^{n+1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j+1)) \]

is an isomorphism. To complete the proof, it suffices to see the equivalence of 5.2.4.2 and 5.2.4.6. For this, let \(a,b\in H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\). By Poincaré duality on \(X\), (5.2.4.6) is equivalent to:
(5.2.4.7) The bilinear form
\[ H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\times H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)) \longrightarrow H^{2n}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(2j+2)), \qquad (a,b)\longmapsto a.L_X(b) \]
is non-degenerate.
But by (5.2.5) and the projection formula, we have
whence the equivalence of (5.2.4.7) and (5.2.4.2).

blocks.title: Corollary 5.2.5.
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2.5. Let \(X\) be projective and smooth, of dimension \(n\), and \(\tau:Y\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\) the inclusion of a smooth hyperplane section. Suppose that \(X\) satisfies the condition (LV). Then for \(i\geq n\), the arrow
\[ \tau^*:H^i(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\longrightarrow H^i(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)) \]

is surjective.
Proof. By (5.2.4.3), we have, for \(i\geq0\)
whence the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=4em@R=4em{ H^{n-i-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j+1))\ar[r]^{L_X^i}& H^{n+i-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j+i+1))\ar[d]^{\tau^*}\\ H^{n-i-2}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[u]^{\tau_*}\ar[r]_{L_Y^{i+1}}& H^{n+i}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j+i+1)),} \]
which gives the desired result, qed.

blocks.title: Lemma 5.2.6.
blocks.html: Lemma 5.2.6. Let \(f:F\longrightarrow B\) be a proper smooth morphism, with \(B\) connected. Let us denote by \(\mathcal O_F(1)\) a relatively ample module on \(F\), and let us equip each geometric fiber \(F_{\bar b}\) with the ample module induced by \(\mathcal O_F(1)\). Then, if one of the geometric fibers satisfies the condition (LV), the same holds for all the others.

Proof. Let us denote by \(\xi_F\in H^2(F,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\) the first Chern class of \(\mathcal O_F(1)\). This class, and its powers \(\xi_F^i\in H^{2i}(F,\mathbb Z_\ell(i))\) induce endomorphisms by cup-product
\[ \tag{5.2.6.1}\xi_F^i:R^{n-i}f_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\longrightarrow R^{n+i}f_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j+i) \]

where \(n\) denotes the relative dimension of the fibers. By the base change theorem for a proper morphism [SGA 4 XII 5.2], all the geometric fibers satisfy the condition (LV) if and only if all the arrows (5.2.6.1) are isomorphisms. Now, by the specialization theorem [SGA 4 XVI 2.2], the sheaves \(R^if_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\) are twisted constant, so that the arrows (5.2.6.1) are isomorphisms if and only if they are so above a given geometric point, qed.

blocks.title: Corollary 5.2.7.
blocks.html: Corollary 5.2.7. Let \(X\) be projective and smooth. If one smooth hyperplane section satisfies (LV), the same holds for every smooth hyperplane section.
Proof. Indeed, the universal family of smooth hyperplane sections of \(X\) satisfies the hypotheses of 5.2.5.
5.3.1. Let
\[ \rho:\widetilde X\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1 \]
be the "fibration" associated with a pencil whose axis cuts \(X\) transversally, and whose generic fiber is assumed smooth.
Let us denote by \(U\) a non-empty open set of \(\mathbb P^1\) above which \(\rho\) is smooth, and by
\[ \tag{5.3.1.1}\nu:U\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1 \]
the inclusion. Taking into account that the sheaves \(R^i\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\) are twisted constant above \(U\), one sees that the sheaves
\[ \nu_*\nu^*R^i\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j) \]
are essentially independent of the choice of \(U\), and that the canonical "adjunction" homomorphisms
\[ \tag{5.3.2}R^i\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\longrightarrow \nu_*\nu^*R^i\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j) \]
are also independent of the choice of \(U\).

5.3.3. Let us remark that for an arbitrary pencil of hyperplane sections, the weak Lefschetz theorem implies that for \(i\leq n-2\), the immersion \(\widetilde X\mathop{\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow}\limits^{(f\times\rho)}X\times\mathbb P^1\) gives rise to an isomorphism of sheaves on \(\mathbb P^1\)
\[ R^i\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } H^i(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))_{\mathbb P^1},\qquad i\leq n-2, \]
i.e. \(R^i\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\) is a constant sheaf for \(i\leq n-2\) and, a fortiori, the arrows (5.3.2) are isomorphisms for \(i\leq n-2\).

5.3.4. We know, by the local theory of vanishing cycles and notably the Picard-Lefschetz formula (XV 3.4), that, for a Lefschetz pencil, the homomorphisms 5.3.2 are isomorphisms for \(i\ne n\), and are surjective for \(i=n\).

5.3.5. We say that \(\rho\), or the pencil from which it comes, satisfies the condition \(A\), if all the homomorphisms (5.3.2) are isomorphisms (or, what amounts to the same thing, if they are so for every \(i\), and \(j=0\)).
We shall see below (6.3, 6.4) that this condition \(A\) has a marked tendency to be satisfied.

blocks.title: Lemma 5.4.
blocks.html: Lemma 5.4. If one of the smooth hyperplane sections of \(X\) (hence every one, by 5.2.7) satisfies (LV) (e.g. if \(X\) satisfies (LV), cf. (5.2.4)), and if \(\rho\) satisfies the hypothesis \(A\), then every fiber (including every singular fiber) satisfies (LV), and the sheaves \(R^i\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\) are constant for \(i\ne n-1\).
Proof. Let us denote by
\[ L:R^i\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\longrightarrow R^{i+2}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j+1) \]
the morphism multiplication by \(f^*c_1(\mathcal O_X(1))\in H^2(\widetilde X,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\); fiber by fiber, it is the operation considered in (5.2.1). By hypothesis, we have
an isomorphism above \(U\)
\[ \tag{5.4.1}\nu^*(L^i):\nu^*R^{n-1-i}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\nu^*R^{n-1+i}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j+i) \]
for \(i>0\), every \(j\). Applying the functor \(\nu_*\), we obtain isomorphisms
\[ ag{5.4.2}( u_* u^*)L^i: u_* u^*R^{n-1-i} ho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } u_* u^*R^{n-1+i} ho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j+i), \]
which, taking into account \(A\), are identified with the morphisms
\[ \tag{5.4.3}L^i:R^{n-1-i}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }R^{n-1+i}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j+i). \]
We conclude by 5.3.3.
5.4.4. Let us denote by \(Y\) a smooth fiber of \(\rho:\widetilde X\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\). By "weak Lefschetz", the restriction arrow
\[ H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\xrightarrow{\ m^*\ }H^{n-1}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)) \]

is injective, and we have seen (5.2.4) that, if \(X\) satisfies (LV), the cup-product on \(H^{n-1}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\), restricted to \(m^*H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\), remains non-degenerate. The orthogonal of \(H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\) in \(H^{n-1}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\) is thus a subspace complementary to \(H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\) in \(H^{n-1}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\), which is called, following the classical terminology, the vanishing cohomology space of \(Y\). One defines a corresponding subsheaf of \(\nu_*(R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\), where \(\nu:U\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\) is an open set of smoothness \(\ne\varnothing\) of \(\rho\), the "vanishing cohomology sheaf" \(E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\) on \(\mathbb P^1\), by

\[ \tag{5.4.4.1}E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)=\nu_*\left\{ \begin{array}{c} \text{the orthogonal in }\nu^*R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\text{ of the}\\ \text{constant subsheaf }H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))_U \end{array}\right\}. \]
It is clear that this sheaf is independent of the choice of \(U\).

blocks.title: Lemma 5.5.
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5. If \(X\) satisfies (LV) and if the arrows (5.3.2) are isomorphisms for \(i=n-1\), for example if \(\rho\) comes from a Lefschetz pencil (cf. (5.3.4)), we have a direct sum decomposition
\[ \tag{5.5.1}R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\simeq H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))_{\mathbb P^1}\oplus E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j). \]
Proof. By (LV), we have such a decomposition above \(U\):
\[ \tag{5.5.2}\nu^*R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\simeq H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))_U\oplus\nu^*E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j), \]
to which we apply \(\nu_*\).

blocks.title: Theorem 5.6.
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.6. Let \(X\) be a projective, smooth and irreducible variety which satisfies (LV) (5.2.2). Let \(\{H_t\}\) be a pencil of hyperplanes whose generic element and axis cut \(X\) transversally, in \(X_\eta\) and in \(\Delta\) respectively. Suppose that the projection \(\rho:\widetilde X\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\) satisfies the condition (5.3.2). Then:

(5.6.1) the Leray spectral sequence

\[ E_2^{p,q}=H^p(\mathbb P^1,R^q\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell)\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell) \]

degenerates. (NB. This remains valid if, instead of supposing that \(X\) satisfies the condition (LV), we only suppose that a smooth hyperplane section \(Y\) satisfies it.)

5.6.2. Let us denote by \(\eta\) the generic point of \(\mathbb P^1\), by \(\bar\eta\) the geometric generic point, by \(X_{\bar\eta}\) the geometric generic fiber of \(\rho\), and by \(\Pi\) the Galois group of \(k(\bar\eta)/k(\eta)\).
For \(q\ne n-1\), we have canonical isomorphisms:

\[ \tag{5.6.2.1}E_2^{0,q}\;(=H^0(\mathbb P^1,R^q\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell))\simeq H^q(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell) \] \[ \tag{5.6.2.2}E_2^{2,q}\;(=H^2(\mathbb P^1,R^q\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell))\simeq H^q(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)) \] \[ \tag{5.6.2.3}E_2^{p,q}\;(=H^p(\mathbb P^1,R^q\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell))=0,\quad p\ne0,2. \]
For \(q=n-1\), we have

\[ \tag{5.6.2.4}E_2^{0,n-1}\;(=H^0(\mathbb P^1,R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell)) \simeq H^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)^\Pi \simeq H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell), \] \[ \tag{5.6.2.5}E_2^{2,n-1}\;(=H^2(\mathbb P^1,R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell)) \simeq H^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(-1))^\Pi \simeq H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)). \]
(The missing value \(E_2^{1,n-1}\) will be found in 5.7 below).

Proof. Theorem 1.1 applies, by (5.4.3), to give (5.6.1). As for the first three formulas (5.6.2), they follow from the fact that the \(R^q\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\) for \(q\ne n-1\) are constant (5.4); for (5.6.2.3) one must of course use that \(\mathbb P^1\) is simply connected. The first

equality of (5.6.2.4) follows from the fact that \(R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\xrightarrow{\sim}\nu_*\nu^*R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\), so that we have \(H^0(\mathbb P^1,R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell)\simeq H^0(U,\nu^*R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell)\), and from the fact that \(\nu^*R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\) is a "twisted constant" sheaf on \(U\), hence equivalent to the \(\Pi\)-module \(H^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\). The second equality of (5.6.2.4) follows from the degeneration, taking into account 5.1.6.

Let us pass to (5.6.2.5). Let us denote by \(F^\bullet\) the filtration defined on \(H^*(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) by the spectral sequence. By degeneration, \(F^2H^{n+1}(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)=E_2^{2,n-1}\), and \(\operatorname{gr}_F^1H^{n+1}(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)=E_2^{1,n}=0\) (5.6.2.3), so that (cf. 5.1.0)

\[ \tag{5.6.3}E_2^{2,n-1}=F^1H^{n+1}(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell) =\operatorname{Ker}\bigl(H^{n+1}(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\longrightarrow H^0(\mathbb P^1,R^{n+1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell)\bigr) \] \[ \text{(by (A))}=\operatorname{Ker}\bigl(k^*:H^{n+1}(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\bigr). \]
By (5.1.2), \(k^*\) can be rewritten
\[ \tag{5.6.4}H^{n+1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\oplus H^{n-1}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)) \xrightarrow{\ m^*+h_*\ }H^{n+1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell), \]
and, by (5.2.2.3), can be rewritten again

\[ \tag{5.6.5}\operatorname{Prim}^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1))\oplus H^{n-3}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(-2))\oplus H^{n-1}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)) \xrightarrow{\ m^*L+m^*L^2,h_*\ }H^{n+1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell). \]
Now, the arrow
\[ H^{n-3}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(-2))\xrightarrow{\ m^*L^2\ } H^{n+1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell) \]

is an isomorphism, by (LV), for \(m^*L_X^2=L_{X_{\bar\eta}}^2\cdot m^*\) (cf. (5.2.4) and (5.2.5)). Hence the kernel of (5.6.5) is identified (by \(\operatorname{pr}_1\oplus\operatorname{pr}_3\)) with
\[ \operatorname{Prim}^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1))\oplus H^{n-1}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)). \]
By (LV),
\[ H^{n-1}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1))\xleftarrow[\sim]{\ L\ } H^{n-3}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(-2))\xleftarrow[\sim]{}H^{n-3}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(-2)), \]
the last by weak Lefschetz. By (5.2.2.3), we thus have
and the other equality of (5.6.2.5) is deduced by "twisting" (5.6.2.4).
Remark 5.6.7. One can prove (5.6.1) under distinctly weaker hypotheses, as DELIGNE explained to me4.

blocks.title: Theorem 5.6.8.
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.6.8. Let \(X\) be smooth of dimension \(n\) over an algebraically closed field \(k\), and
\[ \rho:X\longrightarrow\mathbb P_k^1 \]

a projective morphism, whose generic fiber is smooth. Suppose that:

(5.6.8.1) the geometric generic fiber, equipped with the restriction of the relatively ample module \(\mathcal O_X(1)\), satisfies the condition (LV);

(5.6.8.2) the arrows (5.3.2) are isomorphisms for \(i\ne n\), and are surjective for \(i=n\) (a condition satisfied in particular if \(\rho\) comes from a Lefschetz pencil (cf. 5.3.4)). Then the Leray spectral sequence

\[ E_2^{p,q}:H^p(\mathbb P^1,R^q\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell)\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell) \]

degenerates.
Remark: The same theorem holds, with the same proof, for \(\mathbb P_k^1\) replaced by an arbitrary smooth irreducible projective curve.

Proof. We shall identify, for the proof, the sheaf \(\mathbb Q_\ell(1)\) with \(\mathbb Q_\ell\) (possible since we have taken \(k\) algebraically closed). We may obviously assume \(X\) connected, and \(\rho\) surjective, hence flat, with fibers of dimension \(n-1\). Let us denote by
\[ \xi_X\in H^2(X,\mathbb Q_\ell) \]
the first Chern class of \(\mathcal O_X(1)\). Let
\[ \nu:U\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1 \]
be the inclusion of an open set of smoothness of \(\rho\). By 5.6.8.1 and 5.2.6, the cup-product arrows
\[ \xi_X^i:\nu^*R^{n-1-i}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\longrightarrow \nu^*R^{n-1+i}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell \]
are isomorphisms, for \(i\geq1\), so that, applying the functor \(\nu_*\), we find isomorphisms (taking into account 5.6.8.2) for \(i\geq2\)
\[ \tag{5.6.8.3}\xi_X^i:R^{n-1-i}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\xrightarrow{\sim} R^{n-1+i}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell, \]
and, for \(i=1\), an isomorphism
\[ \tag{5.6.8.4}\xi_X:R^{n-2}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\xrightarrow{\sim} \nu_*\nu^*R^n\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell. \]
This last arrow factors as
\[ \tag{5.6.8.5}R^{n-2}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\xrightarrow{\ \xi_X\ } R^n\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\xrightarrow{\ \text{adjunction arrow}\ } \nu_*\nu^*R^n\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell, \]
so that we have a canonical decomposition
\[ \tag{5.6.8.6}R^n\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell \xleftarrow[\sim]{\ \xi_X+\mathrm{ind}\ } R^{n-2}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\oplus\operatorname{Local}^n\mathbb Q_\ell \]
where we have set
\[ \tag{5.6.8.7}\operatorname{Local}^n\mathbb Q_\ell= \operatorname{Ker}\bigl(R^n\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\longrightarrow \nu_*\nu^*R^n\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\bigr). \]

We note that the sheaf \(\operatorname{Local}^n\mathbb Q_\ell\) is concentrated at the points of \(\mathbb P^1\) where the fiber of \(\rho\) is not smooth, so that, taking (5.6.8.3) into account again, we find
\[ \tag{5.6.8.8}H^j(\mathbb P^1,R^{n-2-i}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell) \xrightarrow{\ \xi_X^i\ }H^j(\mathbb P^1,R^{n-1+i}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell) \quad\text{if }i,j\geq1. \]
Let us now prove the degeneration. Taking into account that \(E_2^{p,q}=0\) except for \(p=0,1,2\), it suffices to prove that the operations
\[ d_2:H^0(\mathbb P^1,R^i\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell)\longrightarrow H^2(\mathbb P^1,R^{i-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell) \]
are zero. For \(q\leq n-1\), let us set
\[ \tag{5.6.8.9}\operatorname{Prim}(E_2^{p,q})= \operatorname{Ker}\bigl(\xi_X^{n-q}:E_2^{p,q}\longrightarrow E_2^{p,2n-q}\bigr). \]
Thanks to (5.6.8.3) and (5.6.8.6), implying the injectivity of
\[ \xi_X:H^p(\mathbb P^1,R^{n-2}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell)\longrightarrow H^p(\mathbb P^1,R^n\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell), \]
for \(q\leq n-1\), we have
\[ \tag{5.6.8.10}E_2^{p,q}\xleftarrow[\sim]{\ 1+\xi_X+\xi_X^2+\cdots\ } \operatorname{Prim}(E_2^{p,q})\oplus\operatorname{Prim}(E_2^{p,q-2}) \oplus\operatorname{Prim}(E_2^{p,q-4})\oplus\cdots. \]
To see that we have
\[ d_2^{p,q}=0\quad\text{for }q\leq n-1, \]
let us simply remark that the commutative diagram

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} \operatorname{Prim}(E_2^{p,q}) & \xrightarrow{\ \xi_X^{n-q}=0\ } & E_2^{p,2n-q}\\ \big\downarrow d_2^{p,q} && \big\downarrow d_2^{p,2n-q}\\ E_2^{p+2,q-1} & \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ \xi_X^{n-1-(q-1)}\ } & E_2^{p+2,2n-q-1} \end{array} \]

(the bottom arrow being an isomorphism thanks to (5.6.8.8)), implies that \(d_2^{p,q}\) kills \(\operatorname{Prim}(E_2^{p,q})\) for \(q\leq n-1\), and hence, by (5.6.8.10), that \(d_2\) kills \(E_2^{p,q}\) for \(q\leq n-1\). By (5.6.8.3), this also implies that \(d_2\) kills \(E_2^{p,q}\) for \(q\geq n+1\). Hence it remains to prove that the homomorphism
is zero, or, what amounts to the same thing, that
We have already proved that we have
and, for reasons of degree,
\[ \tag{5.6.8.14}E_3^{p,q}=E_\infty^{p,q}\quad\text{for every pair }(p,q). \]
\[ b_i(X)=\dim H^i(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)=\sum_{p+q=i}\dim E_3^{p,q}, \]
and, taking (5.6.8.13) into account, we have

By Poincaré duality, we have
Let us first remark that, by (5.6.8.8), we have
so that (by 5.6.8.17) the proof of (5.6.8.12) is complete once the following is known

Now, these two formulas follow from (5.6.8.2) and Poincaré duality for the smooth fibers of \(\rho\), taking into account the following lemma, applied to \(\mathbb P^1\) and to the sheaves \(R^{n-3}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\) and \(R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell\).

blocks.title: Lemma 5.6.9.
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.6.9. (cf. SGA 5 I § 5 and SGA 4 XVIII ). Let \(D\) be a proper, smooth and connected curve over an algebraically closed field \(k\). Denote by \(i:\eta\longrightarrow D\) the inclusion of the generic point, and by \(F\) a constructible \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaf on \(D\) such that \(F\xrightarrow{\sim}i_*i^*F\). Then
\[ H^2(D,F)\xrightarrow{\sim}H^0(D,\check F(-1))^\vee, \]

where \(\check F\xrightarrow{\sim}i_*((i^*F)^\vee)\).

blocks.title: Corollary 5.6.10.
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.6.10. (Uses Picard-Lefschetz). Let \(\rho:\widetilde X\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\) be the «fibration» coming from a Lefschetz pencil, and suppose that the smooth fibers satisfy condition (LV). Then
\[ \tag{5.6.10.1}H^i(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)^\pi= \operatorname{Im}\bigl(H^i(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\longrightarrow H^i(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\bigr). \]
Proof. By 5.3.4 (which uses the Picard-Lefschetz formula), 5.6.8 applies, and gives
\[ H^i(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)^\pi=\operatorname{Image}H^i(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell). \]

It remains to see that \(H^i(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) and \(H^i(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) have the same image in \(H^i(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\). Now, this is true for \(i\leq n-1\) by 5.1.6, and for \(i\geq n\) by 5.2.5.
Let us now pass to the most important computation:

blocks.title: Theorem 5.7.
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.7. Under the hypotheses of 5.6, setting
\[ E^{n-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))=\text{orthogonal in }H^{n-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)) \text{ of the image of }H^{n-2}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)) \]

(the «vanishing» part of the cohomology of \(\Delta\), cf. 5.4.4), we have a decomposition into a direct sum (cf. 4.2)

\[ \tag{5.7.1}E_2^{1,n-1}=H^1(\mathbb P^1,R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell) \xleftarrow[\ f^*+i_*g^*\ ]{\sim} \operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\oplus E^{n-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)). \]
Proof. By degeneration we have
\[ E_2^{1,n-1}\simeq F^1H^n(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)/F^2H^n(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell). \]
By \(A\) we have:
\[ \tag{5.7.2}F^1H^n(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)= \operatorname{Ker}\bigl(k^*:H^n(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\longrightarrow H^n(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\bigr), \]
(by 5.1.2) can be rewritten \(\bigl(H^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\oplus H^{n-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1))\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ m^*+h_*\ }H^n(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\).
By (LV), \(k^*\) can again be rewritten
\[ \tag{5.7.3}\operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\oplus H^{n-2}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)) \oplus H^{n-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)) \xrightarrow{\ m^*+m^*L+h_*\ }H^n(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell), \]
and the middle arrow
\[ \tag{5.7.4}H^{n-2}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1))\xrightarrow{\ m^*L\ }H^n(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell) \]
is an isomorphism (cf. 5.2.3 and (5.2.2.3)), so that, denoting by
\[ \tag{5.7.5}\wp:H^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell) \]
the projection onto the primitive part, we have
\[ \tag{5.7.6}F^1H^n(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell) \xrightarrow[\ \wp f_*\oplus(-g_*L^{2*})\ ]{\sim} \operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\oplus H^{n-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)). \]
By (5.6.2.2) and degeneration, we have
\[ \tag{5.7.7}F^2H^n(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\xrightarrow{\sim}E_2^{2,n-2} \xrightarrow{\sim}H^{n-2}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)). \]

On the other hand, the inclusion of \(F^2H^n(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)=H^{n-2}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(-1))\) in \(H^n(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) is by the arrow \(k_*\) of Gysin. (Strictly speaking, the Gysin arrow is only defined as an arrow
\[ H^{i-2}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\longrightarrow H^i(X_{\overline{k(\eta)}},\mathbb Q_\ell), \]
but taking into account that we have a canonical isomorphism (SGA 4 XII 5.4) for every algebraically closed field \(K/k\)
\[ H^i(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\longrightarrow H^i(X\otimes_kK,\mathbb Q_\ell), \]
we hope that the reader will permit us such abuses.) By (5.1.3), the Gysin arrow is expressed by
\[ \tag{5.7.8}H^{n-2}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)) \xrightarrow{\ m_*\oplus(-h^*)\ }H^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\oplus H^{n-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)). \]
By weak Lefschetz,
\[ m_*H^{n-2}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(-1))=m_*m^*H^{n-2}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)) =L_XH^{n-2}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)) \]
is the orthogonal complement of the primitive part, so that we have the commutative diagram

\[ \tag{5.7.9} \begin{array}{ccc} F^2H^n(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)&\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow&F^1H^n(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\\ \big\uparrow\rlap{\scriptstyle\sim}\,k_*&&\big\downarrow\rlap{\scriptstyle\sim}\,\wp f_*\oplus(-g_*L^{2*})\\ H^{n-2}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(-1))&\xrightarrow{\ 0\oplus h^*\ }& \operatorname{Prim}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\oplus H^{n-2}(\Delta,\mathbb Q_\ell(-1)); \end{array} \]
which indeed gives (5.7.1), qed.
Remark 5.7.10. In 5.7, the hypothesis \(A\) can be replaced by the weaker one that, for \(q=n\), the arrows
\[ \tag{5.7.11}H^q(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\longrightarrow H^0(\mathbb P^1,R^q\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell) \]
and
\[ \tag{5.7.12}H^q(\widetilde X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\longrightarrow H^q(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell) \]
have the same kernel, as our proof shows. In the absence of this condition, the conclusion may fail, cf. 5.9.
5.8. The Griffiths homomorphism.
5.8.1. Let \(f:T\longrightarrow S\) be a morphism of schemes. The Leray spectral sequence defines a filtration \((F^i)\) on the \(H^\bullet(T,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\). We define
\[ \tag{5.8.1.1}\operatorname{Prim}^\bullet(T/S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))=F^1H^\bullet(T,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)). \]
Thanks to the fact that the Leray spectral sequence is contained in the «first quadrant», we have
\[ \tag{5.8.1.2}E_\infty^{0,\bullet}\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow E_2^{0,\bullet}, \]
so that
\[ \tag{5.8.1.3}\operatorname{Prim}^\bullet(T/S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))= \operatorname{Ker}\bigl(H^\bullet(T,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\longrightarrow H^0(S,R^\bullet f_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\bigr). \]
For the same reason, we have
\[ \tag{5.8.1.4}E_\infty^{1,\bullet}\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow E_2^{1,\bullet}, \]
The composite

\[ \tag{5.8.1.5}\operatorname{Prim}^i(T/S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))=F^1H^i(T,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)) \longrightarrow \operatorname{gr}^1H^i(T,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))=E_\infty^{1,i-1} \lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow H^1(S,R^{i-1}f_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))=E_2^{1,i-1} \]
will be denoted
\[ \tag{5.8.1.6}u^i:\operatorname{Prim}(T/S,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\longrightarrow H^1(S,R^{i-1}f_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)). \]

5.8.2. Let now \(X\) be projective, smooth, and irreducible, \(D=\{H_t\}\) a pencil of hyperplanes whose generic element and axis cut \(X\) trans- versally. Denote by \(\nu:U\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\) a nonempty open set of smoothness of the projection \(\rho:\widetilde X\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\) and let \(\widetilde X|U=\widetilde X\times_{\mathbb P^1}U\). We have the commutative diagram
\[ \tag{5.8.2.1}\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ &\widetilde X\ar[dl]_f\ar[dd]^(.3)\rho&\widetilde X|U\ar[l]_\alpha\ar[dd]^{\rho|U}\\ X&&\\ &\mathbb P^1&U\ar[l]^\nu}. \]
The morphism \(\rho|U:\widetilde X|U\longrightarrow U\) being proper and smooth, we have, by the theorem on specialization of cohomology (SGA 4 XVI 2.2)

\[ \tag{5.8.2.2}\operatorname{Prim}^\bullet(\widetilde X|U/U,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)) =\operatorname{Ker}\bigl(H^\bullet(\widetilde X|U,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)) \longrightarrow H^\bullet(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\bigr), \]
hence, by (5.2.3.1), we have

Proposition 5.8.2.3.

Under the hypotheses (5.8.2), for \(x\in H^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\), we have the equivalence

\[ \tag{5.8.2.4}x\in\operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)) \Longleftrightarrow \alpha^*f^*(x)\in \operatorname{Prim}^n(\widetilde X|U/U,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)). \]

If moreover \(\rho\) satisfies the hypothesis \(A\) of 5.3, we have

\[ \tag{5.8.2.5}x\in\operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)) \Longleftrightarrow f^*(x)\in\operatorname{Prim}^n(\widetilde X/\mathbb P^1,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)). \]

Proof. (5.8.2.4) is proved. As for (5.8.2.5), the hypothesis \(A\) implies that \(H^0(\mathbb P^1,R^n\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(U,\nu^*R^n\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\), hence [\(f^*(x)\) is primitive rel. to \(\mathbb P^1\)] \(\Rightarrow\) [\(\alpha^*f^*(x)\) is so rel. to \(U\)].
Definition 5.8.3. In the situation 5.8.2, the composite homomorphism

\[ \tag{5.8.3.1}\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ \operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[r]^{\alpha^*f^*}\ar[dr]_{\operatorname{Grif}}& \operatorname{Prim}^n(\widetilde X|U/U,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[d]^u\\ &H^1(U,\nu^*R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))} \]
is called the Griffiths homomorphism (for \(X\), relative to the given pencil \(D\)).
Let us add to the diagram (5.8.2.1) the constant family over \(U\), with fiber \(X\)

\[ \tag{5.8.3.2}\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ &\widetilde X\ar[dl]_f\ar[dd]^(.3)\rho&\widetilde X|U\ar[l]_\alpha\ar[r]^{f\times\rho|U}\ar[dd]^{\rho|U}&X\times U\ar[ddl]^{\operatorname{pr}_2}\\ X&&&\\ &\mathbb P^1&U\ar[l]^\nu&}. \]

Theorem 5.8.4.

Suppose that \(X\) satisfies (LV) (5.2.2), and that \(\rho\) satisfies the hypothesis \(A\) (5.3). Then for every \(U\xrightarrow{\nu}\mathbb P^1\), open set of smoothness \(\ne\varnothing\) of \(\rho\), the Griffiths homomorphism «modulo fixed part» which is, by definition, the composite

\[ \tag{5.8.4.1}\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ \operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[r]^{\operatorname{Grif}}& H^1(U,\nu^*R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[d]\\ &H^1(U,\nu^*E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\simeq \dfrac{H^1(U,\nu^*R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))} {(f\times\rho|U)^*H^1(U,H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))_U)}} \]

is injective.
Proof. By (5.5.1), we have, over \(\mathbb P^1\),
\[ \tag{5.8.4.2}R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\simeq H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))_{\mathbb P^1}\oplus E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j), \]
and over \(U\), we have
\[ \tag{5.8.4.3}\nu^*R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\simeq H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))_U\oplus\nu^*E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j), \]
which allows us to rewrite (5.8.4.1) as indicated:

\[ \tag{5.8.4.4}\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ \operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[r]^{\operatorname{Grif}}& H^1(U,\nu^*R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[d]\\ &H^1(U,\nu^*E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)).} \]
By (5.7.1), the arrow (which is only defined thanks to the hypothesis \(A\))
\[ \tag{5.8.4.5}u:\operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j)) \longrightarrow H^1(\mathbb P^1,R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell) \]
is injective. The decomposition (5.8.4.2), taking into account that \(\mathbb P^1\) is simply connected, gives
\[ \tag{5.8.4.6}H^1(\mathbb P^1,R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell) \simeq H^1(\mathbb P^1,E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)), \]
so that we can complete (5.8.4.4) into a commutative diagram

\[ \tag{5.8.4.7}\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ \operatorname{Prim}^n(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[r]^{\operatorname{Grif}}\ar[d]_u& H^1(U,\nu^*R^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[d]\\ H^1(\mathbb P^1,E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j))\ar[r]^{\nu^*}& H^1(U,\nu^*E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)).} \]
It now suffices to see that the bottom arrow is injective, which follows from the fact
\[ \tag{5.8.4.8}E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j)\simeq \nu_*\nu^*E^{n-1}\rho_*\mathbb Q_\ell(j) \]
by the following (well-known!) lemma:

\[ \tag{5.8.5.1}H^1(B,\nu_*F)\longrightarrow H^1(A,F) \]

is injective.
